core_takeaway: Agent_1 secured Quill, but the same anchor consensus protected Agent_5's higher-valued Jewel, so the last position won.
why_or_counterexample: The first speaker shaped Quill as an anchor, but the final stable deal gave Agent_5 Jewel, a larger private value than Agent_1's Quill.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion quickly stabilized around Quill for Agent_1 and Jewel for Agent_5, while Pencil and Stone absorbed most of the conflict.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking is available. Agents repeatedly treated Quill and Jewel as protected anchors and focused strategy on rearranging the remaining contested items.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Formal proposals moved from rejected early splits toward the stable allocation Quill-1, Pencil-2, Stone-3, Apple-4, Jewel-5; Agent_5's final proposal won unanimity.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private voting is available. Votes enforced top-item vetoes: Agent_5 rejected losing Jewel, Agent_1 rejected losing Quill, and both accepted proposals preserving those anchors.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflection is available through rounds 1-5. Reflections emphasize anchor stability, Pencil/Stone friction, and limited compensation rather than any durable first-position edge.

mechanisms.theme: Anchor framing protected both first and last
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the opening to frame Quill as the key claim, but Agent_5 immediately made Jewel its own top claim. The repeated anchor logic protected both positions instead of creating a one-sided first-position advantage.

evidence.quote: it solidifies Quill (my top item) in Round 1

mechanisms.explanation: The final deal gave each agent one item, but Agent_5's protected item was worth 61 to Agent_5 while Agent_1's protected item was worth 47 to Agent_1. Equal-looking allocation therefore translated into a last-position utility advantage.
Agent_1's own private planning treated Jewel-to-Agent_5 as part of the stable path. That helped Agent_1 keep Quill, but it also locked in the last agent's superior item.

evidence.quote: Jewel with Agent_5) to lock in my top item (Quill)
Keep Jewel with Agent_5 and Quill with Agent_1 as anchors.
Round 6 baseline Path A: Quill-1, Pencil-2, Stone-3, Apple-4, Jewel-5

mechanisms.theme: Consensus rewarded stability over first control
mechanisms.explanation: Early proposals did not pass when they failed to satisfy enough agents. The winning outcome was the stable anchor-preserving allocation, and it was Agent_5's proposal that reached unanimous acceptance.

The private votes show both end agents defending their top-item claims. Agent_5's Jewel veto was especially strong because losing Jewel destroyed its main payoff, so the process preserved the last agent's best item.

evidence.quote: Jewel (my top item, value 61) is not allocated to me
Proposal 2 gives me Jewel (61) in Round 1
Jewel to Agent_1 instead of Quill; reduces my utility to 28
Moves Quill away from Agent_1 to Agent_2
